walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Champions of Midgard set up. First, unfold the main game board and place it in the center of the table. This board represents the Viking village of Midgard, with various locations around the edges where you will place your workers. You will see spots for the harbor, the marketplace, worker spaces, and monster locations.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Monster Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the monsters. There are three types of monster cards, and you need to separate them into three decks. Troll cards have a green border and these are placed face up in the village. Draugr cards have a blue border and these go near the sea journey area. Journey monster cards have a purple border and are the most valuable. Shuffle each deck separately and place them on their marked spots on the board.

walkthrough.title: Place the Troll
walkthrough.text: Flip over the top card of the troll deck and place it face up on the troll attack space. This troll is threatening the village right now. If nobody defeats it by the end of the round, every single player gets a blame token worth negative three victory points. A new troll will appear each round, so keep your eye on this threat.

walkthrough.title: Place the Draugr and Journey Monsters
walkthrough.text: Flip over two draugr cards and place them face up near the longship docks. These are the sea monsters you can fight by taking a journey. Then flip over two journey monster cards and place them face up near the distant lands space. Journey monsters are worth the most victory points but require more preparation to reach.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Marketplace
walkthrough.text: Find the rune cards and shuffle them into a face-down deck. Reveal a number of rune cards based on your player count and place them in the marketplace area of the board. These runes provide special ongoing abilities you can purchase during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Gather all the resource tokens and sort them into piles near the board. You will need piles for gold coins, food tokens, and wood tokens. Also set out the Favour of the Gods tokens. These are special tokens that let you re-roll dice during combat. Make sure everyone can reach the supply.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Warrior Dice
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize the warrior dice. There are three types and each has a different color. Swordsmen are red dice and they are balanced with a mix of attack and defense. Spearmen are yellow dice and they are more offensive with extra attack faces. Axemen are black dice and they hit the hardest but have zero defense faces. Sort them by color into three pools near the board.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Destiny Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the Destiny cards and shuffle them into a face-down deck. Place this deck near the sea journey area of the board. When players embark on sea journeys, they will draw Destiny cards that can bring positive events like extra resources or negative events like storms that destroy food or warriors. These add excitement and risk to every voyage.

walkthrough.title: Choose Leader Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the leader cards and deal one to each player, or let players draft them if you prefer. Each leader card gives you a unique asymmetric ability that will shape your strategy for the entire game. Read your leader ability carefully before starting. Some leaders are better at combat, some at resource gathering, and some at specific monster types.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board and three wooden worker meeples in their chosen color. Place your three workers on your player board. You will also start with some basic resources. Give each player a starting set of gold, food, and one swordsman die as shown on the setup reference card.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on space one of the round track on the board. The game lasts eight rounds total. Each round, every player will place their three workers one at a time in clockwise order. After all workers are placed and actions resolved, the round ends and a new troll is revealed.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player randomly and give them the first player marker. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. On your turn, you place one worker on an available action space, then the next player places one worker. This continues until all players have placed all three of their workers. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Champions of Midgard is played over eight rounds. Each round, players take turns placing their three workers one at a time on action spaces around the board. After all workers are placed, you resolve combat, clean up, and advance to the next round. The goal is to earn the most victory points by defeating monsters, collecting gold, and avoiding blame tokens.

walkthrough.title: Worker Placement Phase
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you pick up one of your three workers and place it on any unoccupied action space on the board. Most spaces can only hold one worker, so getting to the space you want before someone else does is critical. Play passes clockwise, and each player places one worker per turn. This continues until every player has placed all three workers.

walkthrough.title: Action Spaces: Gathering Resources
walkthrough.text: Several action spaces let you collect resources. You can visit the smokehouse to gain food, the merchant ship to gain gold, or the lumberyard to collect wood. Food is essential for sea journeys because you must feed your warriors on the voyage. Gold lets you buy runes and equipment. Wood is needed to build longships for sea travel. Each resource space typically gives you two or three of that resource.

walkthrough.title: Action Spaces: Recruiting Warriors
walkthrough.text: To fight monsters, you need warrior dice. Several action spaces let you recruit warriors. The barracks lets you take swordsmen, which are the red balanced dice. The blacksmith lets you take axemen, the black high-attack dice. Other spaces let you recruit spearmen, which are the yellow offensive dice. Remember, you are taking actual dice, and these dice are your army.

walkthrough.title: Action Spaces: The Marketplace
walkthrough.text: The marketplace lets you spend gold to buy rune cards. Rune cards give you powerful ongoing abilities for the rest of the game. Some runes give you extra warriors each round, some give bonus victory points for certain actions, and some improve your combat rolls. Buying runes early is an investment that pays off over many rounds.

walkthrough.title: Action Spaces: Fighting the Troll
walkthrough.text: One action space lets you assign a worker to fight the current troll threatening the village. This is important because if nobody defeats the troll by the end of the round, every player at the table receives a blame token worth negative three victory points. When you place a worker here, you will fight the troll during the combat phase using whatever warrior dice you have collected.

walkthrough.title: Action Spaces: Sea Journeys
walkthrough.text: The longship dock lets you embark on a sea journey to fight draugr or journey monsters. To go on a journey, you need a ship and enough food to feed your warriors during the voyage. When you place a worker on the dock, you choose which monster to sail toward. During the journey, you will draw Destiny cards that can help or hurt you before the actual battle.

walkthrough.title: Building and Using Ships
walkthrough.text: Before you can take a sea journey, you need a longship. One action space lets you spend wood to build a ship. Ships are one-time use, meaning each voyage requires a new ship. You also need food for the journey. The food requirement depends on the distance to the monster. Make sure you have both a ship and food before committing to a sea voyage.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Rolling Warrior Dice
walkthrough.text: When combat happens, you roll all the warrior dice you have collected. Each die has four possible results. A sword icon counts as one hit of damage. A shield icon counts as one point of defense. A blank face is a miss and does nothing. A sword-and-shield icon counts as both one hit and one defense. You total up all your swords to determine your attack damage.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Defeating Monsters
walkthrough.text: Each monster card shows its health and its attack value. To defeat a monster, your total sword hits must equal or exceed its health. If you deal enough damage, you claim the monster card as victory points. If you do not deal enough damage, the monster survives and you still suffer its counterattack. Either way, the monster fights back.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Monster Counterattack
walkthrough.text: After you roll, the monster counterattacks. The monster's attack value is the amount of damage it deals to you. For each point of damage you cannot block with shields, you must lose one warrior die permanently. This is not just for this fight. Those dice are gone for good, returned to the supply. Losing warrior dice is one of the most punishing things in the game, so always bring enough defense.

walkthrough.title: Favour of the Gods
walkthrough.text: Favour of the Gods tokens are your safety net in combat. You can spend one Favour token to re-roll any number of your warrior dice during a single combat. This can turn a devastating loss into a victory. You earn Favour tokens from certain action spaces and rune cards. Save them for critical battles where losing dice would cripple your army.

walkthrough.title: Sea Journey: Destiny Cards
walkthrough.text: When you embark on a sea journey, you draw one or more Destiny cards before combat begins. Destiny cards can be positive, granting you extra warriors, food, or gold for the voyage. They can also be negative, destroying food, killing warriors, or forcing you to discard resources. This randomness makes sea journeys risky but potentially very rewarding.

walkthrough.title: Blame Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about blame tokens because they can wreck your score. At the end of each round, if the troll was not defeated by any player, every single player receives one blame token. Each blame token is worth negative three victory points at the end of the game. Over eight rounds, ignoring trolls could cost you up to negative twenty-four points. Someone at the table needs to handle the troll each round.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After all workers are placed and combat is resolved, the round ends. All workers return to their owners. Discard any defeated monster cards that were claimed. Reveal a new troll card for the next round. Refill any empty draugr and journey monster slots. Advance the round marker one space. Then start the next round with a new worker placement phase.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the eighth round, the game is over. Add up your victory points from all defeated monster cards. Then add any bonus points from your leader card ability. Add victory points from collected gold coins, with every five gold worth one point. Finally, subtract three points for each blame token you have. The player with the most victory points wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most remaining warrior dice wins.

walkthrough.title: Monster Set Bonuses
walkthrough.text: There is one more important scoring element. If you collect sets of different monster types, you earn bonus victory points. Having one of each type, a troll, a draugr, and a journey monster, earns you bonus points. Multiple complete sets earn even more. This incentivizes fighting a variety of monsters rather than focusing on just one type.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Midgard
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Champions of Midgard! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first two rounds with four example players. Player 1 through Player 4 will each make different choices, and one of them will make some classic beginner mistakes so you can learn what to avoid. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: The Board Is Set
walkthrough.text: The board is ready with a troll threatening the village, two draugr at the docks, and two journey monsters in the distant lands. Each player has three workers and their starting resources. A troll with four health is staring you down. Remember, if nobody defeats it by the end of the round, everyone gets a negative three point blame token.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Recruit Swordsmen
walkthrough.text: Player 1 starts by placing a worker on the barracks to recruit two swordsmen dice. Swordsmen are the red dice with a good balance of swords and shields. This is a solid opening move because you need warriors before you can fight anything. Player 1 now has three swordsmen total including the one from setup.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Gather Food
walkthrough.text: Player 2 places a worker on the smokehouse to collect food. Player 2 is thinking ahead to a sea journey later. You need food to feed your warriors during a voyage, so stocking up early is smart planning. Three food tokens go into Player 2's supply.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Grab Gold
walkthrough.text: Player 3 heads to the merchant ship to collect gold. Gold is versatile. You can use it to buy powerful rune cards from the marketplace, and leftover gold is worth victory points at the end of the game. Player 3 takes three gold coins.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: The First Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 sends a worker to fight the troll with only their single starting swordsman die. One die against a troll with four health is almost impossible to win. Player 4 rolls and gets a single sword. That is one damage against a monster that needs four. The troll is not defeated and Player 4's swordsman takes the counterattack. With no shields rolled, the die is lost permanently.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Bring Enough Warriors
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is critical. Never send too few warrior dice to a fight. When you lose a combat, you take the monster's counterattack, and every unblocked point of damage kills a die permanently. Player 4 just lost their only warrior for nothing. Always recruit enough warriors before picking a fight. Two or three dice is the minimum for most trolls.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Second Worker Placements
walkthrough.text: Back to Player 1, who places a second worker to recruit two spearmen. Player 2 places on the lumberyard to get wood for building a ship. Player 3 visits the marketplace and spends gold to buy a rune card that grants a bonus warrior each round. These are all smart investments for the early game.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 Recovers
walkthrough.text: Player 4, now without any warrior dice, places a worker on the barracks to recruit fresh swordsmen. It is a recovery move, but it costs an action that could have been used for something more productive. Losing that die early set Player 4 behind by a full turn. The lesson is still ringing. Bring enough warriors to the fight.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Third Worker Placements
walkthrough.text: Player 1 places their third worker on the troll attack space. With three swordsmen and two spearmen, Player 1 is well-equipped for this fight. Player 2 uses their final worker to build a longship with the wood they gathered. Player 3 recruits axemen for their dice pool.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Second Mistake: Ignoring the Troll
walkthrough.text: Player 4 has fresh warriors now but decides to save them for a bigger fight next round instead of helping with the troll. This seems logical but it is a trap. Player 1 is already fighting the troll, so maybe it will be fine. But what if Player 1 fails? Everyone gets the blame token.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Player 1 vs the Troll
walkthrough.text: Player 1 rolls five warrior dice against the troll. The results are three swords, one shield, and one blank. Three damage against a troll with four health. Player 1 does not defeat the troll! The troll counterattacks and Player 1 blocks one damage with the shield but loses one swordsman permanently to the unblocked damage. Now nobody has defeated the troll.

walkthrough.title: Blame for Everyone
walkthrough.text: Since the troll survived, every player at the table receives a blame token worth negative three victory points. That includes Player 4 who chose not to help. If Player 4 had also sent warriors to fight the troll as backup, there would have been a better chance of defeating it. The blame token stings everyone equally.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Never Ignore the Troll
walkthrough.text: Blame tokens are brutal. Negative three points might not sound like much, but over eight rounds they add up fast. If the troll is not defeated two or three times, that is six to nine points lost. Someone always needs to handle the troll, and sometimes it takes more than one player sending warriors. Coordinate with your group or at least pack enough dice to handle it yourself.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 Begins
walkthrough.text: A new round starts with a fresh troll revealed. Everyone has their three workers back. Player 2 has been planning carefully. They have food, a ship, and some warriors. This round, Player 2 is going on a sea journey to fight a valuable draugr.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Sets Sail
walkthrough.text: Player 2 places a worker on the longship dock and chooses to sail toward a draugr. They spend their ship and load up food for the voyage. Before combat begins, Player 2 draws a Destiny card. It is a favorable wind granting one bonus spearman die for this fight. Lucky break! Player 2 now rolls their warriors with the extra die and defeats the draugr, earning solid victory points.

walkthrough.title: Why Sea Journeys Matter
walkthrough.text: Sea journeys are where the big points live. Draugr and journey monsters are worth significantly more than trolls. But they require preparation. You need to gather food, build a ship, and recruit enough warriors. Player 2 spent Round 1 preparing and Round 2 executing. That is the rhythm of a good Champions of Midgard strategy.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Third Mistake: No Ship
walkthrough.text: Player 4 looks at the juicy journey monsters and wants to sail, but they never built a ship. They spent Round 1 losing a warrior to a bad troll fight and then recovering. Now in Round 2, while Player 2 is sailing and earning points, Player 4 cannot access the sea at all. They are stuck fighting the lower-value troll again.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Ship Early
walkthrough.text: If you want to fight the high-value sea monsters, you need to plan at least one round ahead. Build or acquire a ship in one round so you can sail the next. The most valuable monsters in the game are across the sea, and you cannot reach them without a ship. Do not wait until round four or five to start building. Early ship investment opens up the best scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Redemption
walkthrough.text: Player 4 learns from all three mistakes. This round they recruit warriors with their first worker, build a ship with their second, and gather food with their third. They are setting up perfectly for a sea journey in Round 3. No more rushing into fights underprepared, no more ignoring threats, and no more missing the boat.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through two rounds of Champions of Midgard and seen all the key mechanics in action. You have watched Player 4 learn the hard way that you should never send too few warriors to a fight, never let the troll go undefeated, and always prepare a ship early for sea journeys. You are ready to play a full eight-round game on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to warrior dice, monster types, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May the gods favor your battles!

summary.title: Before You Sail
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for dice, monsters, terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, rushing into combat with too few warrior dice and losing them permanently. Second, ignoring the troll and accumulating blame tokens that cost you negative three points each. Third, not building a ship early enough and missing out on high-value sea monsters. Fourth, hoarding resources without ever fighting monsters. Remember, warrior dice in your supply score zero points. You need to use them to defeat monsters and earn victory points.

walkthrough.title: Warrior Dice Selection
walkthrough.text: Understanding when to use each type of warrior die is key. Swordsmen are your red dice and they are the safest pick. They have a good mix of swords, shields, and sword-plus-shield faces. Bring swordsmen when you expect heavy counterattacks. Spearmen are yellow and lean offensive. Use them when you need raw damage and the monster's counterattack is manageable. Axemen are black dice with the highest attack potential but zero shields. Only bring axemen when you absolutely need to kill a tough monster and you have shields covered by other dice or Favour tokens.

walkthrough.title: Troll Management
walkthrough.text: The troll is the most important recurring decision in the game. Every round, someone needs to defeat it or everyone suffers. Here is a good mindset: think of troll duty as a shared tax. If you always let others handle it, you will eventually get burned when nobody steps up. If you fight the troll yourself, you at least control the outcome. Budget one or two workers per round toward troll management, whether that means recruiting warriors to fight it or actually sending someone in. Two or three swordsmen with a Favour token backup is usually enough.

walkthrough.title: Resource Timing
walkthrough.text: Champions of Midgard rewards planning ahead by one round. You cannot recruit warriors and fight in the same round because both require worker placements. So you need to recruit warriors in round one to fight in round two, gather food and build a ship in one round to sail the next. Think of each round as setting up the next. The best players always know what they want to do next round and spend this round preparing for it.

walkthrough.title: Favour of the Gods Usage
walkthrough.text: Favour tokens are some of the most valuable resources in the game, but only if you use them wisely. Do not spend a Favour token on a troll fight where the stakes are low. Save them for sea journeys against high-value monsters where losing the fight means wasting your ship, food, and potentially your warrior dice. A Favour re-roll can turn a failed sea journey into ten or more victory points. Collect Favour tokens whenever you can and treat them as insurance for your most important battles.

walkthrough.title: Sea Journey Preparation
walkthrough.text: Sea journeys are the path to victory but they require significant investment. You need a ship, food, and enough warrior dice to actually win the fight. Plan your sea journeys at least one full round ahead. In the preparation round, build a ship and stockpile food. In the execution round, recruit any final warriors and set sail. Do not try to squeeze all the preparation into one round because you will not have enough worker placements to do everything.

walkthrough.title: Monster Set Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Do not overlook the set bonus scoring. Collecting one troll, one draugr, and one journey monster gives you bonus victory points beyond what each card is worth individually. This means you should diversify your monster hunting. A player who defeats three trolls and nothing else will score less than a player who defeats one of each type. After your first troll kill, prioritize getting a draugr and a journey monster to complete a set. Multiple complete sets multiply the bonus.

walkthrough.title: Leader Card Synergy
walkthrough.text: Your leader card shapes your entire strategy, so lean into it. If your leader gets bonuses for defeating certain monster types, prioritize those monsters. If your leader grants extra resources, use those resources aggressively to take more actions. Some leaders reward specializing in one dice type while others reward variety. Read your leader card at the start and build your whole game plan around it. A player who fights against their leader's strengths will always lose to someone who embraces theirs.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't rush into combat with too few dice — permanent die loss
Don't ignore trolls — blame tokens = -3 VP each
Don't skip ship building — sea monsters are highest VP
Don't hoard resources — unspent warriors score zero

summary.title: Warrior Dice Selection
bullets: Swordsmen (red) — balanced, best for heavy counterattacks
Spearmen (yellow) — offensive, use when defense is manageable
Axemen (black) — max damage, zero defense, use as finishers
Mix dice types based on monster's attack value

summary.title: Troll Management
bullets: Troll duty = shared responsibility every round
Budget 1-2 workers per round toward troll defense
2-3 swordsmen + Favour token backup usually enough
Letting troll go undefeated = -3 VP for EVERYONE

summary.title: Resource Timing
bullets: Plan 1 round ahead — recruit warriors now, fight next round
Gather food + build ship in one round → sail the next
Each round sets up the following round's actions

summary.title: Favour of the Gods Usage
bullets: Save Favour tokens for high-value sea battles, not low-stakes trolls
One re-roll can swing 10+ VP on a journey monster fight
Collect Favour whenever possible — treat as combat insurance

summary.title: Sea Journey Preparation
bullets: Sea journeys need: ship + food + warrior dice
Prep round: build ship + stockpile food
Execution round: recruit final warriors + set sail
Don't try to squeeze all prep into one round

summary.title: Monster Set Bonuses
bullets: 1 troll + 1 draugr + 1 journey monster = set bonus VP
Diversify monster hunting — don't just farm trolls
Multiple complete sets = multiplied bonus
After first troll, prioritize draugr and journey monsters

summary.title: Leader Card Synergy
bullets: Lean into your leader's strengths — build strategy around it
Leader bonuses for specific monsters → prioritize those
Leader resource bonuses → use aggressively for extra actions
Fighting against your leader's strengths = losing strategy

walkthrough.title: Troll Specialist Strategy
walkthrough.text: One viable approach is becoming the dedicated troll fighter. Invest heavily in swordsmen for their defensive shields and fight the troll every single round. This strategy works because troll cards are relatively easy to defeat, you protect the entire table from blame tokens which earns goodwill, and you steadily accumulate troll cards for set bonuses. The key is efficiency. If you can defeat a troll with two or three dice and minimal losses, you free up your other workers for resource gathering and sea journey prep.

walkthrough.title: Sea Raider Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach is to focus almost entirely on sea journeys. Journey monsters are worth the most victory points in the game, and a player who successfully completes three or four sea journeys will usually win. The plan is to spend rounds one and two gathering food, building ships, and recruiting a strong dice pool. Then sail every round from round three onward. The risk is Destiny cards, which can sink your plans. Always carry extra food and keep a Favour token in reserve for bad luck.

walkthrough.title: Rune Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: An underrated approach is to invest heavily in rune cards during the first three rounds. Some runes grant you a free warrior die every round, which over five or six remaining rounds adds up to massive value. Other runes give bonus victory points for specific actions you would take anyway. The rune engine strategy sacrifices early combat power for exponential late-game returns. Spend gold aggressively on runes in rounds one through three, then dominate rounds four through eight with your accumulated advantages.

walkthrough.title: Dice Pool Management
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think carefully about their dice pool composition. A balanced pool of three swordsmen, two spearmen, and one axeman gives you flexibility for any fight. But sometimes specialization is better. An all-swordsmen pool maximizes your chance of surviving counterattacks, while a spearmen-heavy pool maximizes damage output. Match your dice pool to your strategy. Troll specialists want swordsmen for survivability. Sea raiders want spearmen and axemen for maximum damage to defeat high-health monsters in one round.

walkthrough.title: Blame Token Math
walkthrough.text: Understanding blame token economics separates good players from great ones. Each blame token is negative three points. If another player spends two workers and three warrior dice to kill a troll, they spent significant resources to save everyone from a negative three penalty. That is actually a bad deal for the troll fighter and a great deal for everyone else. The advanced play is to only fight the troll when it is efficient for you, and to let blame tokens accumulate when the cost of preventing them exceeds three points of value. Of course, this only works if you are ahead on other scoring.

walkthrough.title: Worker Placement Denial
walkthrough.text: Since most action spaces can only hold one worker, blocking your opponents from key spaces is a powerful tactic. If you see an opponent preparing for a sea journey, taking the dock space before them wastes their preparation. If someone needs warriors badly, occupying the barracks forces them to find alternatives. The best blocking moves are ones that benefit you while also denying your opponent. Going first in a round is hugely valuable because you get first pick of all open spaces.

walkthrough.title: Destiny Card Risk Management
walkthrough.text: Sea journeys are high variance because of Destiny cards. Advanced players manage this risk in several ways. First, always carry more food than the minimum requirement so a negative Destiny card does not end your journey. Second, keep a Favour token for re-rolls in case the monster fight goes badly. Third, consider the timing of your voyages. Earlier journeys give you more time to recover from bad luck, while late-game journeys leave no recovery time. Fourth, some leader cards and runes mitigate Destiny card risk, so prioritize those if you plan to sail often.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Optimization
walkthrough.text: The final two rounds are where games are won and lost. By round seven, stop investing in long-term assets like runes and start maximizing immediate victory points. Convert remaining gold into scoring opportunities. Fight every monster you can reach. Complete any monster sets you are close to finishing. If you have been accumulating warrior dice all game, rounds seven and eight are when you cash them in for monster kills. A player who hoards dice but never fights will always lose to a player who fights efficiently throughout the game.

summary.title: Troll Specialist Strategy
bullets: Fight the troll every round — protect table from blame tokens
Invest in swordsmen for defensive shields
Efficient troll kills free workers for other actions
Steady troll accumulation feeds set bonuses

summary.title: Sea Raider Strategy
bullets: Focus on sea journeys — journey monsters = highest VP
Rounds 1-2: gather food, build ships, recruit warriors
Rounds 3+: sail every round, 3-4 successful voyages usually wins
Carry extra food + Favour tokens for Destiny card risk

summary.title: Rune Engine Strategy
bullets: Buy runes aggressively in rounds 1-3
Free warrior/round runes = massive cumulative value
Sacrifice early combat for exponential late-game power
Dominate rounds 4-8 with accumulated rune advantages

summary.title: Dice Pool Management
bullets: Balanced pool (3 swords, 2 spears, 1 axe) = flexibility
All-swordsmen = max survivability for troll specialists
Spearmen + axemen heavy = max damage for sea raiders
Match dice composition to your overall strategy

summary.title: Blame Token Math
bullets: Each blame = -3 VP, but fighting trolls costs resources
Sometimes letting blame accumulate is correct (if ahead elsewhere)
Troll fighters spend resources to save everyone — often a bad deal for them
Only works when your other scoring exceeds the blame penalty

summary.title: Worker Placement Denial
bullets: Block opponents from key spaces (dock, barracks)
Best blocks = benefit you AND deny opponent
Going first in a round = huge advantage
Watch opponents' preparations to identify blocking opportunities

summary.title: Destiny Card Risk Management
bullets: Carry more food than minimum for negative Destiny cards
Keep Favour token in reserve for bad combat rolls
Early voyages allow recovery time; late voyages don't
Prioritize leader cards/runes that mitigate journey risk

summary.title: Late Game Optimization
bullets: Rounds 7-8: stop investing, start cashing in
Convert gold into scoring, fight every monster possible
Complete any nearly-finished monster sets
Hoarded dice = wasted potential — spend them on monster kills

entries.definition: The balanced warrior die. Faces include swords, shields, sword-plus-shield combos, and blanks. Best for fights where you expect significant counterattack damage and need defensive shields.
The offensive warrior die. Has more sword faces than the swordsman die but fewer shields. Best for fights where you need maximum damage output and the monster's counterattack is low or manageable.

The high-risk, high-reward warrior die. Has the most sword faces of any die type but zero shield faces. Only bring axemen when you have shields covered by other dice or Favour tokens, or when you absolutely must kill a tough monster.
A die result that counts as one hit of damage against a monster. Total swords rolled must equal or exceed the monster's health to defeat it.
A die result that blocks one point of the monster's counterattack damage. Each unblocked damage point kills one warrior die permanently.
A die result that counts as both one hit of damage and one point of defense simultaneously. The best possible roll on any warrior die.
A die result that does nothing — no damage dealt and no defense provided. Blanks are the worst possible roll and are the main reason Favour tokens exist.
Village-threatening monster that appears each round. If undefeated at round end, all players receive a blame token. Generally lower VP value but essential to handle to avoid blame penalties.
Undead sea monsters found on ocean voyages. Worth more VP than trolls. Requires a ship and food to reach. Two draugr are available at a time, replaced when defeated.

The most valuable monster type, found in distant lands across the sea. Worth the most VP but requires the most preparation: ship, food, and strong warrior dice pool. Two journey monsters available at a time.
The number of sword hits required to defeat a monster. Printed on the monster card. If your total swords are less than the health value, the monster survives.
The amount of counterattack damage a monster deals after you roll. Each point of damage not blocked by a shield kills one warrior die permanently.
After rolling warrior dice, the monster fights back with its attack value. Damage not blocked by shields causes permanent warrior die loss. Happens whether you defeat the monster or not.

When a warrior die is killed by unblocked counterattack damage, it is returned to the general supply and gone for the rest of the game. This is the most punishing mechanic and the reason defensive planning matters.

A special token that lets you re-roll any number of your warrior dice during a single combat. Earned from specific action spaces and rune cards. Best saved for critical sea battles against high-value monsters.
Currency used to buy rune cards from the marketplace. Leftover gold at game end is worth victory points, typically five gold equals one VP. Earned from the merchant ship action space.

Required resource for sea journeys. You must load food onto your ship to feed warriors during the voyage. Earned from the smokehouse action space. Insufficient food means you cannot embark on a journey.
Resource used to build longships. Ships are required for sea journeys to fight draugr and journey monsters. Earned from the lumberyard action space.
A ship built by spending wood at the shipwright action space. Required for sea journeys. Each ship is single-use — one voyage consumes it. Build ships in advance to sail the following round.
Action space where you place a worker to recruit swordsmen (red warrior dice). One of the primary warrior recruitment locations.
Action space where you place a worker to recruit axemen (black warrior dice). Provides the highest-damage dice but with zero defense.
Action space where you place a worker to gather food tokens. Food is essential for sea journey preparation.
Action space where you place a worker to collect gold coins. Gold is used to purchase rune cards and scores VP at game end.
Action space where you place a worker to collect wood tokens. Wood is spent to build longships for sea journeys.
Action space where you spend gold to buy rune cards. Rune cards provide powerful ongoing abilities for the rest of the game.
Action space where you place a worker to embark on a sea journey. Requires a ship and food. You choose which draugr or journey monster to fight.
Action space where you place a worker to fight the current round's troll. If nobody occupies this space and defeats the troll, all players receive a blame token.

A card drawn during sea journeys before combat begins. Can be positive (bonus warriors, extra food, gold) or negative (storms destroying food, killing warriors, forcing resource loss). Adds risk and excitement to every voyage.
A favorable Destiny card that grants bonuses such as extra warrior dice for the fight, additional food, gold, or other resources. Makes the sea journey more likely to succeed.
An unfavorable Destiny card representing storms or misfortune at sea. Can destroy food (potentially ending your journey), kill warrior dice before combat, or force you to discard resources.

A penalty token worth negative three victory points at game end. All players receive one whenever a troll goes undefeated at the end of a round. Over eight rounds, accumulated blame tokens can cost a player the game.

Bonus victory points earned for collecting complete sets of monster types: one troll, one draugr, and one journey monster per set. Multiple complete sets earn increasing bonuses. Incentivizes fighting diverse monster types.

A unique card giving each player an asymmetric ability for the entire game. Leaders may grant bonuses for specific monster types, extra resources, improved combat, or special scoring conditions. Your leader should guide your overall strategy.

Purchasable cards from the marketplace that provide ongoing abilities. Some grant free warriors each round, bonus VP for certain actions, or combat improvements. Early rune investment compounds over many rounds.
The primary scoring currency. Earned from defeated monsters, monster set bonuses, leader card bonuses, and leftover gold. Blame tokens subtract VP. Highest total VP wins the game.
The tracker on the board marking which of the eight rounds is currently being played. A marker advances one space each round. The game ends after round eight.

Each player's three wooden figures placed on action spaces during the worker placement phase. All workers return to players at the end of each round. Workers are placed one at a time in clockwise order.
